summary: Nadia examines Handshake's approach of giving 70% of its value to developers as a superior alternative to traditional platform developer funds. Unlike Slack or Facebook funds that take equity from developers building on their platforms, Handshake rewards developers with ownership stakes. She argues this better aligns incentives between platforms and third-party developers, potentially avoiding the adversarial relationships seen with Twitter's contentious API policies. The model treats developers as collaborators rather than sources of value extraction.
key_points: Traditional developer funds paradoxically take equity from developers who increase platform value, rather than rewarding them
Giving developers platform equity could reduce friction when platforms inevitably make unfavorable policy changes
Platform-developer relationships have become political battles rather than collaborative partnerships, as seen with Twitter's API restrictions
Developer equity awards may be simpler and cheaper than acquiring and shutting down popular third-party apps
Early contributors to platform success deserve compensation similar to early employees whose skills become less central as companies grow
money_quotes: Far from altruistic, by giving away ownership to incentivize development, Handshake seems like a better version of the 'developer funds' offered by platforms.
Developers who build popular apps on a platform arguably increase the company's valuation. In exchange for this service, the platform takes equity in their company. (The gall!)
Platform-developer relationships today resemble a political battle more than a dance.
It'd be great if Twitter had been able to reward the apps that made Twitter so popular in the early days, while also doing what Twitter needed to do to innovate.
App developers work with platforms suspiciously, giving them the side-eye instead of being excited to build cool things with it.

I don't see developer equity as a worse option for companies, but rather a way to better align incentive and reward structures, treating developers as collaborators with a stake in the company's future instead of pure value extraction.
key_insight: Platforms should give developers equity stakes rather than extract value from them, transforming an adversarial relationship into genuine collaboration where both sides benefit from mutual success.
